title: Residual Plots: Visualizing Your Model's Errors
concept: A residual plot graphs each prediction's error against its predicted (or actual) value, revealing patterns — like curves or funnels — that a single summary number like RMSE or R² can hide.

exercises.prompt: Recap: Why might a report include RMSE, MAE, and R² together instead of just one metric?

options.text: Because each metric highlights a different aspect of model performance, and no single number tells the whole story
Because they always give identical results
Because only R² is ever accurate

exercises.explain: Right — together they paint a fuller picture. Today: an even fuller picture, in the form of a plot.

cards.title: Same RMSE, very different problems
cards.body: Two models both score RMSE = $40,000. One makes small, random mistakes everywhere. The other is spot-on for cheap houses but wildly wrong for expensive ones. The number can't tell them apart — a picture can.

cards.title: What is a residual plot?
cards.body: A residual is the leftover error for one prediction (actual minus predicted). A residual plot graphs these residuals against the predicted value, so you can see the shape of your model's mistakes, not just their average size.

cards.title: An X-ray for your model
cards.body: RMSE and R² are like a single number on a medical chart — useful, but limited. A residual plot is like an X-ray: it lets you see exactly where and how something is going wrong.

cards.title: A funnel shape
cards.body: If residuals fan out wider as predicted price increases, that funnel shape means the model is far less reliable for expensive houses — a pattern a single RMSE number would completely hide.

cards.title: A good score doesn't mean random errors
cards.body: You might assume a high R² guarantees the model is equally reliable everywhere. It doesn't — a model can post a strong overall score while still being systematically wrong for a specific slice of the data. The residual plot is what exposes that.

cards.title: Healthy vs. unhealthy residuals
cards.body: What the shape of the scatter tells you:

left.text:
No visible pattern
Model is capturing the relationship well

right.text:
Visible shape in the errors
Model is missing something systematic

exercises.prompt: What is a "residual" in a residual plot?

options.text: The leftover error: the actual value minus the model's predicted value
The total number of predictions made

exercises.explain: A residual is simply actual minus predicted — the leftover amount the model didn't capture for that one data point.

exercises.prompt: True or false: A residual plot that shows points scattered randomly around zero, with no visible pattern, is a sign of a healthy model.
exercises.explain: True! Random scatter with no pattern means the model's errors aren't systematically related to the predicted value — a good sign.

exercises.sentence: If residuals fan out into a widening funnel shape as predictions increase, the model's errors are ___, not random.
exercises.explain: A funnel or curve shape means the errors follow a pattern — something systematic the model is missing — rather than being random noise.

exercises.prompt: A residual plot shows a clear U-shaped curve instead of a random scatter. What does this most likely indicate?

options.text: The model is missing a pattern in the data, such as a curved relationship it's currently treating as a straight line
The model has a perfect R² score
The dataset has no errors at all

exercises.explain: A curved pattern in the residuals usually means the model's assumed relationship (often a straight line) doesn't match the true shape of the data.

exercises.prompt: A house-price model posts a strong R² = 0.88 and a reasonable RMSE. But its residual plot shows a widening funnel: small errors for cheap houses, huge errors for expensive ones. What should the team do with this information?

options.text: Investigate why the model struggles specifically with expensive houses — the summary metrics looked fine, but the plot reveals a systematic weakness a single number couldn't show
Ignore the plot, since a strong R² already proves the model is reliable everywhere
Report only the R² score going forward, since visual checks are unnecessary once R² is high
Conclude the model has zero errors, since R² is close to 1

exercises.explain: Exactly! Strong summary metrics can still hide systematic problems in a slice of the data. Residual plots are the check that catches what RMSE and R² alone can miss. That's the toolkit: distance-based metrics, unit conversion, variance explained, metric selection, and visual diagnostics — everything you need to judge a finished regression model.
